author: Saiki77
description: A secure, plug-and-play way to sync individual vault folders or sections with GitHub. Private repos supported, multiple folder mappings, bidirectional sync, and conflict resolution. - This plugin has not been manually reviewed by Obsidian staff.

manifest.author: Saiki77
manifest.description: A secure, plug-and-play way to sync individual vault folders or sections with GitHub. Private repos supported, multiple folder mappings, bidirectional sync, and conflict resolution.
manifest.name: Easy Git

name: Easy Git

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description: v1.6.2: pause toggle for folder mappings

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
fix: never pull remote files that match the exclude list

Exclusions only filtered the local scan, so a path that exists on the remote
but is excluded (for example a .obsidian/app.json that was synced before the
exclusion was added) was still classified as pull-add. The engine then tried
to create it in the vault and failed with "File already exists", because
config-dir and dotfiles are not tracked vault files the create-fallback can
find, so the sync errored on every run no matter what was added to the exclude
list.

Filter the remote file map by the same per-mapping exclude patterns before
classifying, mirroring the local scan, so an excluded path is invisible on
both sides. Verified against the classifier that this adds no spurious delete:
an excluded path already in last-sync state becomes a no-op.

Reported on the community forum. Bump to 1.6.1.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
v1.5.3: clear remaining validator deprecation warnings + release notes

Eliminates the last advisory warnings from the Obsidian plugin
validator and gives every release a description. Built and linted
against the validator's own Obsidian 1.13.0 typings so the result is
verified, not blind.

- display deprecation (4×): the deprecation was on internal
  this.display() re-render calls. Extracted the settings-tab body into
  a private render() method; display() now just delegates to it. The
  display() override stays (required — minAppVersion 1.6.6 predates the
  declarative getSettingDefinitions API), but nothing calls the
  deprecated path anymore.
- setWarning deprecation (3×): added markButtonDestructive() shim that
  calls setDestructive() when present (1.13.0+) and falls back to
  setWarning() otherwise, resolved through a structural cast so the
  deprecated symbol isn't referenced directly. Backward compatible:
  works on every version from 1.6.6 up; nothing breaks for users on
  pre-1.13 Obsidian.
- TS2612: base Plugin now declares `settings` (unknown) in 1.13.0;
  changed our field to `declare settings: PluginSettings` to override
  the type without shadowing.
- Bumped the obsidian dev dependency to 1.13.0 so local build + lint
  match the validator going forward (minAppVersion stays 1.6.6;
  no-unsupported-api still guards against using newer APIs unguarded).
- Release workflow now writes the annotated tag message as the GitHub
  release body, so releases are no longer description-less.

Validator result: source-code check goes from warnings to clean;
dependencies already passed.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 1.4.0 — automatic 3-way merge for conflicts where edits don't overlap

The promise from the 1.3.0 release notes lands here. The conflict pipeline now has three layers; only what survives them lands in the modal:

| Layer | Handles |
| --- | --- |
| **mtime auto-resolve** (1.2.2) | "Local is decisively newer" — single-user multi-device drift |
| **3-way text merge** (this release) | "Both sides edited disjoint regions of the same file" |
| **Conflict modal** (existing) | Anything genuinely ambiguous |

### How the merge works

For each `both-edited` conflict that survives the mtime pre-pass, the engine:

1. Fetches the common-ancestor blob from GitHub at `lastSyncState.files[path].sha` — that's the SHA both sides forked from, so it's the correct merge base.
2. Reads current local content from the vault and remote content (already fetched).
3. Runs a 3-way text diff (using the [node-diff3](https://github.com/bhousel/node-diff3) library, MIT, ~14 KB bundled).
4. If the merge is **clean** (no overlapping edits): backs up the pre-merge local content to `.easy-git-backup/<timestamp>/`, writes the merged content to the vault, and queues a push of the merged file. The conflict modal never opens for this file.
5. If the merge has **hunk-level conflicts** (both sides touched the same line range): skips. The conflict falls through to the modal as before.

A Notice fires noting how many files were auto-merged.

### Where the merge is skipped on purpose

- **Binary files** (PNG, PDF, etc., detected by extension): can't merge image bytes, so they go to the modal.
- **`.md` files in mappings with wikilink rewrite on**: the SHAs we'd be merging are of the rewritten markdown that lives on GitHub, not the wikilink-form vault content. Writing merged rewritten markdown back to the vault would convert wikilinks to image-link syntax in your notes. Modal handles these.
- **Files without a recorded base SHA** (first sync, missing lastSyncState entry): there's no common ancestor to merge against. Modal.
- **Anything that errors during fetch, decode, or merge**: silent fallback to modal — no risk of data loss because the backup mechanism still runs.

### Safety guarantees retained from 1.3.0

The pre-merge backup runs unconditionally for every merge that gets written to disk. If your merge later turns out to be wrong, the original local content is at `.easy-git-backup/<timestamp>/<original-path>` in your vault. Restoring is a copy-back.

If you want to opt out of auto-merging on a specific mapping, set its direction to **pull only** — the merge pass never runs for pull-only mappings (it would be meaningless, since pull-only never pushes back).

### What's bundled

This is the first runtime dependency in the plugin: `node-diff3` (MIT, ~10 KB minified, ~14 KB after bundling with esbuild). `main.js` grew from ~53 KB to ~67 KB. The dependency is reproducible from `package.json` + `package-lock.json` and bundled into the released artifact, so the release verification (`gh attestation verify main.js`) still works the same way.

### What this doesn't include

- **Auto-merging `.md` with wikilink rewrite on.** A future release could attempt the merge on rewritten content, run a reverse-rewrite of the merged result back to wikilinks, and write that to the vault. The round-trip needs careful testing against the rewriter's rules. Not in 1.4.0.
- **Auto-pruning of `.easy-git-backup/`.** The folder still grows over time. Manual cleanup remains the answer for now.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 1.3.0 — local files are never lost

The single guarantee this release adds: **your local content is never destroyed by a sync, unless you've explicitly told the mapping "pull only"** (which is the opt-in for remote-wins behaviour). Every operation that would overwrite or delete a vault file first writes a snapshot of the original to `.easy-git-backup/<timestamp>/<original-path>`.

### What the engine now does, in order

1. **Before any pull-modify**: snapshot the existing local file to the backup folder. Then overwrite with the remote content.
2. **Before any pull-delete**: snapshot the existing local file. Then delete.
3. If the backup write itself fails (disk full, permission error, name collision): **abort the entire sync** with a clear error. No file gets overwritten if we couldn't first save a copy of it.

Backups are skipped only when the mapping's direction is `pull` — there, you've explicitly told the plugin "remote wins, replace local," and tracking every overwrite defeats the point.

### Where backups live

```
<vault>/.easy-git-backup/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHmmss>/<original vault path>
```

One folder per sync run, original path structure preserved inside. Restoring is a copy-back. The folder is **hardcoded** to be excluded from any sync, so backups stay local and never get pushed.

### What this fixes for users

- "I accepted 'keep remote' in the conflict modal and lost a paragraph I'd been working on" — the pre-overwrite snapshot is right there in the backup folder.
- "A remote change came in via a teammate and silently replaced something I had locally" — same.
- "I synced a stale state from another machine and overwrote good local content" — same.

The conflict modal still opens for genuine conflicts; the backup runs underneath every pull operation regardless of how the resolution was reached.

### What's not in this release

- **Auto-pruning of old backups.** The `.easy-git-backup/` folder grows over time. Delete subfolders by hand once you're sure you don't need them. Auto-rotation may come as an opt-in setting later.
- **3-way text merge using GitHub's stored base blob.** I was originally going to bundle this in 1.3.0 but it deserves a proper round of diff3 test fixtures before shipping. Coming as 1.4.0. The idea: for `both-edited` conflicts where the actual diffs don't overlap, the engine auto-merges using the blob at `lastSyncState.baseCommitSha` as the common ancestor, just like a normal git merge. That reduces the conflict modal further. The mtime auto-resolve from 1.2.2 covers the common case in the meantime.

### Notice surface

When backups happen, a Notice fires:

> Easy Git (\<mapping\>): backed up N local file(s) to .easy-git-backup/2026-05-25-150523/ before overwrite.

So you can always tell when overwrites occurred and where to find the original content.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 1.2.2

### Auto-resolve "both-edited" when local was clearly newer

The most common conflict-modal complaint: "I'm the only one editing, my local copy is obviously the right one, why is it asking me?" Now the engine pre-resolves before the modal opens.

For each `both-edited` conflict, the engine compares the local file's current mtime against the mtime recorded in lastSyncState. If local is decisively newer (more than 2 seconds after the recorded mtime — the grace covers filesystem jitter), the resolution is auto-set to **keep local**. If every conflict in a sync run pre-resolves this way, the modal never opens. A Notice fires noting how many were auto-resolved.

This handles the single-user-on-multiple-devices case (where `data.json` drift produces a flood of spurious both-edited conflicts) without removing the modal entirely. Anything ambiguous — mtimes too close to call, missing mtime in lastSyncState, or other conflict kinds like edit-vs-delete — still surfaces in the modal as before.

The other direction (auto-keep-remote) isn't included: we don't have a reliable remote-side mtime signal, and auto-pulling a remote change over a local edit is destructive in a way that needs the user's eyes on it.

### What about real 3-way merging?

On the radar. A proper 3-way text merge (using GitHub's blob at lastSyncState.baseCommitSha as the common ancestor, plus local and remote, fed through diff3) would let the engine resolve hunk-level conflicts inside the same file. That's a bigger change with its own UX questions (what does a partially-merged file look like in the conflict modal?). Not in this release. If users still hit conflicts the mtime pre-pass can't catch, that's the next direction.

### What this doesn't change

- `keep-both` resolutions still need to be picked file-by-file in the modal (renames are always file-specific).
- Push-only mappings still produce conflicts when remote has changes the user didn't expect — the modal opens normally for those.
- The bulk "Apply keep-local / keep-remote to all unset" buttons from 1.2.1 are still there for any remaining ambiguous cases.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 1.2.1

Two fixes from a single user report.

### Fix: spurious "Vault folder no longer exists" error

The safety net introduced in 0.4.0 was being too strict: a trailing or leading slash in the stored vaultFolder path (or a case-insensitive filesystem renaming) made `vault.getFolderByPath` return null even though the folder was right there. When the user re-pointed the mapping to the same folder via the picker, the picker wrote a clean normalized path and the lookup succeeded — masking the real issue.

The fix:
1. **Normalize the lookup**: strip leading/trailing slashes before calling `getFolderByPath`.
2. **Case-insensitive fallback**: when the direct lookup fails, walk the vault folders and match case-insensitively. Common on macOS/Windows.
3. **Auto-recovery**: when the fallback finds the folder, the mapping's saved path is silently updated to match the canonical casing. Subsequent syncs hit the fast path.

The actual safety net (refuse to sync when the folder is genuinely missing) still fires when the folder is truly gone — no regression on the data-loss protection that 0.4.0 introduced.

### Conflict modal: bulk "Apply to all unset" buttons

When a single user works on one folder across multiple devices, their lastSyncState can drift (often via Obsidian Sync racing the plugin's `data.json`). That can produce a long list of "both-edited" conflicts where the user knows the right answer for every one of them: "keep local everywhere" (or "keep remote everywhere"). Currently they have to click through every row.

Now: two new buttons at the bottom of the conflict modal — **Apply keep-local to all unset** and **Apply keep-remote to all unset**. Each one fills in the resolution for every conflict that doesn't already have one chosen. Conflicts that already have a chosen resolution are left alone, so you can fine-tune a few edge cases and bulk-resolve the rest.

`keep-both` isn't offered for bulk because it's almost always file-specific (rename one copy and inspect).

### Note on the underlying both-edited drift

If you're a single user seeing recurring conflicts on a push-only mapping across multiple devices, the most common cause is the plugin's `data.json` (which contains `lastSyncState`) drifting between machines. Easy Git stores its state in `<vault>/.obsidian/plugins/easy-git/data.json` — if you use Obsidian Sync, that file gets synced too, but race conditions between two devices editing it can produce stale state. The bulk-apply buttons make the cleanup one click instead of N.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description: ## 1.2.0 ### Excalidraw drawings now render on GitHub Notes containing wikilink embeds like `![[Drawing.excalidraw|700]]` previously rewrote to a plain link to the raw JSON, which GitHub couldn't render. Easy Git now auto-resolves to the **companion image** that the Excalidraw plugin exports. When the rewriter sees an Excalidraw embed (`.excalidraw` or `.excalidraw.md`), it looks for a sibling `.svg` or `.png` in the same folder and rewrites to point there. SVG is preferred over PNG. Out-of-folder companions get co-located into the mapping's remote `attachments/` folder just like any other out-of-folder image, so the GitHub side stays self-contained. To enable: turn on **Auto-export SVG** (or PNG) in the Excalidraw plugin's settings. Excalidraw then writes a companion `.svg` next to every drawing each time you edit it, and Easy Git picks it up automatically. If no companion exists, the embed falls back to a plain link and a Notice fires telling you how to enable auto-export. ### Width hints preserved as HTML img Previously `![[image.png|700]]` rewrote to `![](image.png)` and dropped the width hint (CommonMark has no width syntax). Now it rewrites to `` so drawings and screenshots keep their intended size on GitHub. Affects all image embeds with numeric width hints, not just Excalidraw. ### Why this took shape Thanks to the issue that surfaced this — embedded Excalidraw drawings in study notes that weren't rendering. The companion-resolution approach reuses Excalidraw's existing export pipeline, so no parsing of Excalidraw's JSON format and no extra dependencies. The plugin stays focused on what it's good at.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 1.1.0

Addresses [the visibility issue from the community](https://github.com/Saiki77/Easy-Git/issues): seeing what's going on when a sync errors with something opaque like `File already exists`.

### Sync log

Every sync run is now recorded in an in-Obsidian log so you don't have to open the developer console to debug. Each entry shows:

- Mapping name and destination
- Trigger (manual, interval, startup, on-save, command)
- Time, duration, outcome
- For successful runs: the files added / modified / deleted
- For failed runs: the full error message including the vault path that caused it

The log keeps the most recent 100 entries and can be cleared from the modal.

Open it via:
- The **View sync log** button next to *+ Add mapping* in settings, or
- Command palette: `Easy Git: Show sync log`

### Better error messages

Errors thrown during pull operations now include the vault path of the file that failed:

> Before: `File already exists.`
> After:  `File already exists. (at "Notes/Foo.md")`

### Case-insensitive file collision recovery

The `File already exists` error itself was usually a case-insensitive filesystem collision (macOS, Windows): the remote has `Foo.md`, the vault has `foo.md`, and `getFileByPath` returns null but `vault.create` then fails because the underlying disk has the file already.

Easy Git now tries to recover automatically: if `vault.create` throws `already exists`, the engine falls back to a case-insensitive lookup, finds the file regardless of casing, and writes via `vault.modify` instead. The error only surfaces if recovery itself fails.

This should fix most reports of *"File already exists, but I can't tell which one"*.

### Thanks

To the user on GitHub who reported the issue with a useful log dump — that turned what would have been a guessing game into a quick fix.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 1.0.0 — stable

The plugin is feature-complete and in the Obsidian community directory. Schema is stable, behaviour is locked in. Breaking changes from here on bump to 2.0.

### What 1.0 ships

**Sync model**
- Per-folder or whole-vault sync between Obsidian and GitHub
- Multiple folder mappings, each with one or more destinations
- Push, pull, or bidirectional per mapping
- Mirror to several repos, fan out to several folders of one repo, or aggregate multiple remote sources into one vault folder — all from the same destinations list
- One atomic commit per sync run via the Git Data API
- Non-fast-forward retry when a sibling pushed mid-run

**Safety**
- Conflict modal prompts on both-sides edits with keep-local / keep-remote / keep-both
- Missing-folder safety net (renamed or deleted vault folder is never interpreted as \"delete every file on the remote\")
- Auto-fix when you rename or move the mapping's folder inside Obsidian
- Sibling-noise filter so multi-source pull doesn't produce noisy notices

**Authentication**
- Personal Access Token (fine-grained or classic)
- Sign in with GitHub via Device Flow OAuth — no token copy-paste
- Tokens stored in vault, no third-party servers

**Markdown**
- Obsidian wikilinks (\`![[image.png]]\`) auto-rewritten to standard CommonMark at push time so GitHub renders them inline
- Out-of-folder attachments co-located into \`attachments/\` under the mapping's remote folder
- Vault files never modified — only the bytes pushed to GitHub are rewritten

**Quality of life**
- Per-mapping \`.easygitignore\` on top of global excludes
- Status bar indicator with click-to-settings
- Sync button stays in \"Syncing…\" state across settings open/close
- Build-provenance attestations on every release artifact

**Compatibility**
- Desktop and Obsidian mobile, same code path
- No shell access, no node-only modules
- Settings migration is automatic and one-way; existing 0.x users see no behavioural change other than the new options being available

### Thanks

To the early users on the forum and Discord who surfaced the use cases — especially the multi-destination pattern and the noisy-pull edge case — that shaped 0.5 and locked this release in as 1.0.

### What's next

Driven by issues and feedback. The plugin is intentionally narrow in scope (folder ↔ GitHub repo); features that would push it toward being a full git client (commit history view, branch management, restore-from-commit) are out of scope.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 0.5.1

Builds on 0.5.0's multi-destination feature to support the **inverse pattern**: many remote folders into one vault folder.

### Many sources → one vault folder

Already works in 0.5.0, just wasn't called out. Set a mapping's direction to **pull**, add multiple destinations, and each destination pulls its own remote contents into the shared vault folder. Use cases:

- Aggregate notes from several team repos into one personal incoming folder
- Subscribe to multiple upstream documentation repos and read them all in one place
- Mirror multiple sources into a single curated vault location

The README now has a dedicated section under \"Multiple destinations per mapping\" documenting this pattern with a diagram.

### Noise fix

In a pull-only multi-destination mapping, each destination scans the shared vault folder and sees files brought in by sibling destinations. Previously each destination would fire an informational Notice listing those sibling files as \"changed locally, not pushed.\" Now sibling-owned paths are filtered out of each destination's view so the notices only mention files this destination actually owns.

Safe: only applies when the mapping's direction is \"pull\" and there are 2+ destinations. The push-mirror case (one vault folder → several repos) is unaffected.

### Path collisions

If two pull destinations bring files with the same path (e.g. both have \`intro.md\` at their root), the second sync wins — whichever destination runs last overwrites the file. Use distinct remote paths or rename on the remote side if both files need to coexist. Detecting this and surfacing a proper conflict will come in a later release.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 0.5.0

A single mapping can now push the same vault folder to **multiple destinations** at once.

### Why

Useful in two scenarios:

- **Mirroring** — the same notes pushed to a public repo AND a private backup repo simultaneously.
- **Static-site fan-out** — one Obsidian folder feeding several subfolders of a website repo, e.g. \`Notes/blog\` → \`src/content/blog\`, \`Notes/projects\` → \`src/content/projects\`, all in the same repo, all from the same vault location.

### What's new

- Each mapping has a **destinations list** instead of a single (repo, branch, path) triple. Add as many as you want.
- Destinations sync **sequentially**, each producing its own atomic commit. If destination 1 errors, destination 2 still tries.
- Each destination tracks **its own last-sync state** — a hiccup with one remote doesn't poison the others.
- The conflict modal title shows the destination repo when a mapping has more than one, so it's clear which target the conflict is for.
- Status bar and mapping-row status aggregate across all destinations (any error → error state, most recent successful sync wins for the timestamp).

### Migration

Existing 0.4.x mappings are converted **automatically** on first load — your settings file transparently gains a \`destinations[0]\` with all the previous fields moved into it. No action required, no data loss, last-sync state preserved.

### Compatibility

No breaking changes from a user perspective. The UI for single-destination mappings looks essentially the same (the destinations section just shows one row). The schema migration is one-way and idempotent.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 0.4.1

Two fixes.

### Sync button persists "Syncing…" across settings open/close

Previously: when you triggered a sync on a large folder and then closed/reopened the settings panel, the Sync button reset to its idle "Sync" label even though the sync was still running in the background. Clicking it again would queue a duplicate (harmless but confusing).

Now: the Sync button and the row's status line read live from the plugin's in-flight sync set. Open settings during a sync and the button shows "Syncing…" (disabled). Close and reopen — still "Syncing…". When the sync finishes, the button flips back automatically.

### Conflict resolutions for delete-vs-edit cases

Two specific conflict kinds resolved as silent no-ops in the previous releases:

- `local-edited-remote-deleted` + Keep remote → should delete the local file. Was emitting a stale `pull-modify` with no source, doing nothing.
- `remote-edited-local-deleted` + Keep local → should push the deletion to remote. Was emitting `push-modify` against a missing local file, erroring.

Both now emit the right deletion operation and the conflict resolves cleanly. No data loss in either previous form — just a confusing UX where the modal said "resolved" but the file states stayed unchanged.

No other behaviour changes vs 0.4.0.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 0.4.0

Three related changes around how the mapping's vault folder is handled.

### Whole-vault sync

The vault root is now pickable in the folder picker — choose it to sync your entire vault to a repo (or a subfolder of a repo) instead of just one folder. The mapping summary shows `Whole vault` for clarity. Existing per-folder mappings work exactly as before.

### Auto-fix on folder rename

If you rename or move a mapping's vault folder inside Obsidian, Easy Git updates the mapping automatically (and any mapping nested inside the renamed folder). A small Notice confirms the update. Works for the file-explorer rename, drag-to-move, and renames-via-command.

### Safety net: missing-folder protection

**Bug fix.** Previously, if the mapping's vault folder was missing on sync (deleted, or moved while Obsidian was closed so no rename event fired), the engine would interpret every previously-synced file as locally-deleted and **push the deletions to the remote** — wiping the remote folder. Now the engine detects the missing folder and aborts the sync with a clear error so nothing is lost.

If you ever see `Vault folder "X" no longer exists`, restore the folder, edit the mapping to point at the new location, or delete the mapping. Sync resumes safely once the path matches an existing folder again.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 0.3.0

Two small quality-of-life additions.

### Status bar indicator

A small element in Obsidian's bottom-right status bar shows aggregate sync state across all mappings:

- `↻ Ready` — at least one mapping configured, nothing has synced yet
- `↻ Synced 5m ago` — last successful sync (most recent across all mappings)
- `↻ Syncing…` — a sync is in progress (violet)
- `! Easy Git error` — at least one mapping has an unresolved error (red)

Click it to jump straight to settings. Hidden when no mappings are configured. Updates every 30 seconds.

### Per-mapping `.easygitignore`

Drop a `.easygitignore` file at the root of any mapping's vault folder. Its patterns are added to the global exclusion list for that mapping only. Same gitignore-style syntax: one glob per line, `#` for comments. Useful when you want to exclude `*.pdf` in one mapping but not another. The `.easygitignore` file itself is never pushed.

No behavior changes vs 0.2.1 for users who don't add a `.easygitignore` or look at the status bar.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## 0.2.1

Polish release for the community directory:

- Build artifacts (`main.js`, `manifest.json`, `styles.css`) are now signed with GitHub artifact attestations, so anyone can cryptographically verify they were built from this source.
- README now documents the clipboard / network / vault permissions the plugin uses.
- Release notes are now part of every tag.

No behavior changes vs 0.2.0. If you're upgrading from 0.2.0 you don't need to do anything; the wikilink rewrite introduced there is unchanged.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## What's new in 0.2.0

- **Wikilink rewrite for GitHub.** Obsidian's `![[image.png]]` embeds now get rewritten to standard CommonMark (`![](image.png)`) at push time so notes render with their images inline on GitHub. Out-of-folder attachments are co-located into `attachments/` under the mapping's remote folder, keeping each remote folder self-contained.
- Per-mapping toggle: **Convert Obsidian wikilinks for GitHub**. New mappings get this on by default. Existing mappings auto-enable on the next sync after upgrade, with a one-time Notice so you know it's happening.
- Section embeds (`![[note#header]]`), block embeds (`![[note^block]]`), and internal `[[note]]` links are left untouched.
- The rewriter respects fenced code blocks and inline code, so wikilinks in example snippets in your notes aren't altered.
- Unresolved wikilinks (pointing at a file that doesn't exist in your vault) stay as-is and surface a Notice with the count.

## Upgrading

Install via BRAT as usual. The first sync after upgrade may produce a larger commit than normal because every `.md` in the mapping gets its wikilinks rewritten in one pass. After that, syncs are minimal again.

## Authentication and conflict handling

Unchanged from 0.1.x. PAT or GitHub Device Flow OAuth, both work for private repos. Conflicts (the same file changed on both sides since the last sync) still prompt with **keep local / keep remote / keep both**.
